# Composing the distributions

## Why composition? A serial interval is built from parts

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- A serial interval is a generation time plus the difference of two
  incubation periods
- Seen through primary and secondary interval censoring, then right
  truncation
- Its hazard might come from a within-host model, or be empirical
- Every model wants a different combination of these parts
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- One observation model, many compositions:
  - GT $+$ incubation difference
  - censoring $\circ$ truncation
  - within-host hazard vs empirical hazard
- We want to express any of them from reusable, validated parts,
  not re-implement each by hand
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
This is the composable-modelling idea applied to delays: build the
serial interval (and other epi delays) by composing small, tested
pieces, so the structure is written down once and reused.
:::

## CensoredDistributions.jl: the parts, today

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
- Plain [Distributions.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStats/Distributions.jl)
  delays are the leaves
- Wrappers add primary censoring, interval censoring and right
  truncation
- `double_interval_censored` composes all three in one call
- The result *is* a distribution: `pdf`, `cdf`, `rand`, and fitting
  with [Turing.jl](https://github.com/TuringLang/Turing.jl)
:::
::: {.column width="52%"}
```julia
using CensoredDistributions, Distributions

# Gamma delay, primary + secondary
# censoring, truncated at 15
d = double_interval_censored(
    Gamma(2, 3); upper = 15, interval = 1)

pdf(d, 4.0)
rand(d, 1000)
```

```julia
using Turing

@model function fit(values, weights)
    α ~ truncated(Normal(1, 2), 0, Inf)
    θ ~ truncated(Normal(1, 2), 0, Inf)
    d = double_interval_censored(
        Gamma(α, θ); upper = 15, interval = 1)
    values ~ weight(d, weights)
end
```
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
[CensoredDistributions.jl](https://github.com/EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl)
gives the censoring and truncation pieces as composable wrappers over
Distributions.jl.
Docs: <https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/>
:::

## In-flight: a language for composing delays

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- A small set of composers that nest inside each other:
  - `Sequential` (additive chain of delays)
  - `Parallel` (branches off a shared origin)
  - `Competing` / `HazardCompeting` (one of several outcomes, by
    probability or by racing hazards)
  - `Select` (a data field picks which sub-model applies)
- One `compose` front-end lowers a `NamedTuple`, a table, or a matrix
  to the same stack, with no monolithic tree type
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- `event_tree` and `event_names` name every event in the record, so a
  whole linelist row scores as one distribution
- `params_table`, `build_priors` and `update` inventory and refit the
  shared parameters, including ties across branches (`shared`)
- `intervene`, `swap_child`, `cut_branch`, `splice` edit a tree
  structurally and return a fresh, valid distribution
- A composed delay can be censored and truncated on the outside, or
  carry censored leaves inside
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
Why it matters: the many forms a serial interval can take, GT plus an
incubation difference, within-host vs empirical hazards, censored or
not, become points in one space of compositions.
This is the latent-delay composed-distributions work in
[PR #363](https://github.com/EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl/pull/363),
built from validated parts and fittable with Turing.jl.
:::
